title: Entity Definitions for TXT Files

Entity Definitions for TXT Files

Entity Definitions for TXT Files

The TXT connector stores entity data in a character string format and it does not support conversion of binary data. Thus, to produce a file that is correctly formatted for the external tool that will use the data, you need to ensure that the corresponding Uniface entity definition, including its fields and interfaces, are correctly defined.

When defining entities and fields for data that will be exported or imported using the TXT connector, follow these guidelines:

  • For a text file with fixed-length fields, define all the fields in the entity with fixed lengths.

  • For a text file with comma-separated fields, define at least one fixed length field, and one or more variable length fields with a comma as a string identifier. See the example Example: Interface Definitions for Comma-Separated Files.

  • For entities with variable length fields , (C*, W*, and so on), ensure that entity data is less 8192 bytes per record. This prevents overflow records from being created, which are not useful for exporting data.

    Tip: If you do not have existing overflow records, you can increase this limit to 32,767 bytes. See Record Structure.

  • Define all fields in the variable part of the record as character string fields. Conversion of binary data is not supported and will generate an error message if a conversion to Unicode is requested.

  • Use the W packing code if you need to be able to predict the field width as a number of Unicode characters.

  • To use Unicode encoding for an existing application and entity with fixed length Text fields using ASCII data, edit the assignment file and use the /uni switch for that entity, or USYS$TXT_PARAMS uni. For more information, see Configuring the TXT Connector and uni.

The following sections provide background information on these guidelines.

Record Structure

When the TXT connector is used for specified entities, Uniface translates entity definitions into records in a text file. Each record is an occurrence of the entity and its contents are determined by the entity's field definitions.

Uniface is based on the relational database model, so each entity should have at least one fixed length field that (in theory) can be used as the primary key. The structure of a record thus consists of a fixed length part, optionally followed by a variable length part. The fixed length part contains all of the fixed length fields up to the first variable length field. The rest of the record is the variable length part of the record.

For entities that contain only fixed-length fields, the maximum record length is 2,147,483,647 bytes.

For entities that contain variable-length fields, the default maximum record size is 8192 bytes. If a record with variable-length fields exceeds 8192 bytes in length, Uniface breaks the record up into a main record and overflow records. The structure of overflow records is not useful for exporting data, so records handled by the TXT driver must be shorter than this.

It is possible to increase the size of a record with variable-length fields to 32,767 bytes. To do so, set the Database Interface property of the entity to C*.32. If necessary, recompile the components that use the entity.

CAUTION: Do not do this if there are existing overflow records. Changing the record length renders these records unreadable.

Data Mapping

Uniface converts fields in the fixed part of the record to character string versions of the data type. For example, an I4 field in the fixed part of the record is always written as a string with N11 packing code, not as a 4-byte binary value. This ensures that only text data is provided and not binary data. However, fields in the variable part of the record are always provided in their defined format. Converting binary data is not supported by the TXT connector, so fields in the variable part of the record must be String fields or have character-based packing codes such as B2, E5, or N1-32. For more information, see TXT Data Packing.

Fields with a W packing code in the fixed part of the record are provided in UTF32 encoding. All other fields in the fixed part of the record are provided in the $DEF_CHARSET. For the variable part of the record, W fields are provided to the driver in UTF8 format, and other fields are provided in their conceptual format. String fields will be in $DEF_CHARSET.

The resulting record must be written to the file in the same encoding, so conversions may be required.

Data Conversion

The TXT connector converts text in different encodings to the desired Unicode encoding in a file. It tries to do this with as few conversions as possible.

For example, if Uniface provides the entity data in UTF8, and the TXT connector has been requested to write this to a file in UTF8, the entire record can be written in one pass without conversions. If the entity definition defines the fixed part of the record in the system character set and the variable part in UTF8, the TXT connector converts the fixed part of the record to UTF8, appends the variable part that is already in UTF8, and writes the record in one pass.

The TXT connector converts the whole record in one pass for the following combinations of fields.

  • All fields are in the fixed part of the record and have a W packing code.

    Conversion: UTF32 to the character set defined for the file.

  • All fields are in the fixed or variable part of the record and have a Text packing code.

    Conversion: $def_charset to the file character set defined for the file.

  • All fields are in the variable part of the record and are a mixture of Text and W packing codes; $def_charset == utf8

    Conversion: UTF8 to the character set defined for the file.

When the fields in an entity are defined with a mixture of W and Text packing codes in the fixed-length part of the entity, conversions must be done per field. Where possible the TXT driver will convert the whole of the fixed part or the whole of the variable part in one go. In the worst case, each field in the fixed part of the record may need to be converted to the desired Unicode individually, before it is written to the file.

The TXT connector writes fixed length W fields as a fixed number of Unicode characters. If a fixed number of bytes is required, the field should be defined as a C field. Unicode can still be used by setting $DEF_CHARSET to UTF8.

Example: Interface Definitions for Comma-Separated Files

When handling comma-separated text files, use the variable-length technique of defining a comma as string identifier. In the following example, which includes data in quotes, you also need to define the quote marks as part of the string identifier.

A comma-separated file contains two records, each containing two strings, a number, and another string:

’string1’,’string2’,1200,’string3’
’string4’,’string5’,123,’string6’

To read or write this file, the field interface definitions in the entity must be as follows:

Table 1. Field Interface Definitions for Quoted Fields

Field

Data

Field Interface

Result

Field1

string1

C*VI/’

’string1

Field2

string2

C*VI/’,’

','string2

Field3

1200

C*VI/’

’,1200

Field4

string3

C*VI/,’

, ’string3

Field5

C*VI/’

'

Field6

C1

The string identifiers in the interface definition are used to prefix the field data with commas and quotes. Field5 is always empty, does not need to be painted on the form, and its function is to provide the closing quote for the data from Field4. Field6 is present because Uniface requires an entity to have a primary key and a fixed-length field is required for that. However, it is possible to define Field6 as Not in database (Is External is F) so that it is not written to the output file.

Related Links
  • Field Interface Codes

    A field interface code is specified in the field's Database Interface property, and determines how the data is stored in the database. The code defines the packing code and field length, as well numeric decimal and scaling options.

Field Interface Definitions for Quoted Fields Field Data Field Interface Result Field1 string1 C*VI/’ ’string1 Field2 string2 C*VI/’,’ ','string2 Field3 1200 C*VI/’ ’,1200 Field4 string3 C*VI/,’ , ’string3 Field5 C*VI/’ ' Field6 C1 The string identifiers in the interface definition are used to prefix the field data with commas and quotes.
